definition: A mathematical transform that decomposes a signal into its constituent frequencies
States that a signal must be sampled at least twice its highest frequency to avoid aliasing
Distortion that occurs when a signal is sampled below the Nyquist rate causing frequency overlap
A mathematical operation describing how a filter modifies a signal in the time domain
The output of a system when the input is a unit impulse; fully characterizes a linear system
The ratio of output to input in the frequency domain describing a system's behavior
A mathematical transform used to analyze discrete-time signals and systems
A mathematical transform converting time-domain signals into the complex frequency domain
The number of samples taken per second when converting a continuous signal to discrete form
The process of mapping continuous amplitude values to a finite set of discrete levels
The ratio of desired signal power to background noise power typically expressed in decibels
A logarithmic unit used to express the ratio of two power or amplitude values

A filter that allows frequencies above a cutoff frequency to pass while attenuating lower ones

A filter that attenuates a narrow band of frequencies while passing all others
The position of a point in time within a waveform cycle expressed as an angle in degrees
The magnitude of a signal representing the peak value from the baseline
A representation of a signal showing how its energy is distributed across frequencies
A representation of a signal showing how its amplitude varies over time

A transform that converts a finite sequence of equally spaced samples into frequency components
The process of varying a carrier signal to encode information for transmission
The process of extracting the original information signal from a modulated carrier

The level of background noise below which a signal cannot be reliably detected
